title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
SEO Insertion on Is the Surrender Value of Endowment Life Insurance Plans Taxable? - Article Page
Keyword density and no. of target keywords has been mentioned as comments in the beginning
Suggested additional anchor texts are marked as comments in Yellow. (If required)
Keywords: endowment policy, endowment plan, surrender an endowment plan
Body Content:
Several Indians have recently realised the benefit of insurance plans, be it health insurance plans or life insurance plans. This was long in coming, but now, compared to previous times, Indians prefer to have peace of mind knowing that they have financial protection when an emergency occurs with their health, or in the event of certain unfortunate circumstances.
In case you wish to have some financial backing for your health costs in the future, health insurance plans are a good bet. Life insurance policies are becoming popular ways to ensure your family’s financial costs are covered should your untimely demise occur. An ideal way to get cover for your life is to sign up for an endowment policy.  An endowment life insurance plan is a special life insurance plan that is a kind of way to allocate your funds to save money. This is a life insurance plan that gives you a risk-free and guaranteed return. All you have to do is make a fixed payment on a monthly basis, and this is your premium.
Hence, this represents a kind of term life insurance, but with a savings scheme attached. In case of your unfortunate demise before the policy reaches its maturity date, your beneficiaries will get a payout as a death benefit. If you survive the term of the endowment plan, you get a maturity payout.
A Lowdown on the Tax Treatment of an Endowment Policy
In many cases, policyholders might not wish to continue with a life insurance plan. This may be for various reasons. Policyholders may fall short of funds, or there may be more suitable alternatives in the market. Whatever the reason for surrender of a policy may be, if a policyholder wishes to give up an endowment policy, it is crucial to be aware of the implications of tax. On amounts you receive as a result of the surrendering.
In the most common of terms, an endowment policy is a non-ULIP. You can get two kinds of endowment plans. One is available with a profit, and the other is available without a profit. The plan that involves “a profit”, gives policyholders bonuses regularly. These are given as a result of your funds being invested in a company and the company making profits. The plans that are available “without a profit” offer you returns that are guaranteed, but no profits as such, as they don’t invest your funds in companies.
If you are making any kind of profit out of an endowment plan, there would be a tax component attached. Among the two kinds of plans mentioned, there are varied conditions like guaranteed plans, pension plans, and money-back plans, among other plans. Before you take the step to surrender an endowment plan, you should know about any tax liability you may have to face.
Taxability
If you have a conventional insurance policy, you are not liable to pay any tax on the surrender value of this kind of policy. This is provided the premiums of the first two years of the policy tenure have been paid in total. Furthermore, the time of issue of the policy matters in relation to taxability. If your conventional life insurance plan was issued before 31 March 2003, the amounts you accrue would be free from any tax liability. If the policy was issued between the period 1 April 2003 and 31 March 2012, then the surrender value would not be liable for any tax provided the assured sum is more than five times the amount of the premium paid annually. For any policies issued on 1 April 2012, or after that date, the surrender value would not incur any tax, provided the assured sum is equal to more than ten times the premium paid annually. Such tax regulations are applicable to any endowment policy too.
If you surrender your policy, there are particular conditions in which amounts are completely taxable. If a sum that is received from a surrendered policy is attained on account of the following, the policyholder is exempt from tax:
If the policy is issued by LIC, or any other insurance company, with a clause for cover of a dependent with a disability, provided the dependent is deceased before the policyholder.
If the policy is issued after 1 April 2012 and the payable premium for any year in the tenure of the policy is above 10% of the assured sum. This applies if the policy issue date is after 1 April 2013 and the covered policyholder has a severe disability as specified by Section 80U. This is applicable even if a person has an ailment or disease as per section 80DDB.
Conclusion
An endowment plan is a sensible way to save money while you assure financial protection for your family and yourself. With insurance plans, you get a variety to suit your needs with different insurers. This is a good way to allocate your wealth, while you get financial protection for your loved ones in your absence. You can avail certain tax advantages too.
full_pdf_text:
Is the Surrender Value of Endowment Life Insurance Plans Taxable? Keywords: endowment policy, endowment plan, surrender an endowment plan Indians prefer to have peace of mind knowing that they have financial protection when a health related emergency occurs, or in the event of certain unfortunate circumstances. In case you wish to have some financial backing for your expenses in the future, insurance plans are a preferred bet. Life insurance policies are becoming popular ways to ensure your family’s financial costs are covered should your untimely demise occur. One of the preferred way to get cover for your life is to consider signing up for an endowment policy. An endowment life insurance plan is a life insurance plan which helps the policyholder save regularly over a specific period of time so that he/she is able to get a lump sum amount on the policy maturity in case he/she survives the policy term. Hence, this represents a kind of term life insurance, but with a savings scheme attached. In case of your unfortunate demise before the policy reaches its maturity date, your nominee(s) will get a payout as a death benefit. If you survive the term of the endowment plan, you get a maturity payout as per policy terms & conditions. A Lowdown on the Tax Treatment of an Endowment Policy In many cases, policyholders might not wish to continue with a life insurance plan. This may be for various reasons. Policyholders may fall short of funds, or there may be more suitable alternatives in the market. Whatever the reason for surrender of a policy may be, if a policyholder wishes to give up an endowment policy, it is crucial to be aware of the implications of tax. On amounts you receive as a result of the surrendering. You can get two kinds of endowment plans. One is available with a profit, and the other is available without a profit. The plan that involves “a profit”, gives policyholders bonuses, if declared by the company. These are given as a result of your funds being invested in a company and the company making profits. The plans that are available “without a profit” offer you returns that are guaranteed, but no additional bonuses as such. If you are making any kind of profit out of an endowment plan, there would be a tax component attached. Before you take the step to surrender an endowment plan, you should know about any tax liability you may have to face. Taxability If you have a conventional insurance policy, you are not liable to pay any tax on the surrender value of this kind of policy. This is provided the premiums of the first two years of the policy tenure have been paid in total. Furthermore, the time of issue of the policy matters in relation to taxability. If your conventional life insurance plan was issued before 31 March 2003, the amounts you accrue
would be free from any tax liability. If the policy was issued between the period 1 April 2003 and 31 March 2012, then the surrender value would not be liable for any tax provided the death sum assured is equal to or more than five times the amount of the premium paid annually. For any policies issued on 1 April 2012, or after that date, the surrender value would not incur any tax, provided the death sum assured is equal to or more than ten times the premium paid annually. Such tax regulations are applicable to any endowment policy too. If a sum that is received from a surrendered policy is attained on account of the following, the policyholder is exempt from tax:
● If the policy is issued after 1 April 2003 but on or before 31 March 2012 and the payable premium for any year in the tenure of the policy is equal to or less than 10% of the death sum assured. ● If the policy is issued after 1 April 2012 and the payable premium for any year in the tenure of the policy is equal to or less than 20% of the death sum assured. Conclusion An endowment plan is a sensible way to save money while you assure financial protection for your family and yourself. There are many insurance plans to choose from, so you can find one that fits your needs. You may take aid of a life insurance calculator for further assistance. This is a preferred way to allocate your wealth, while you get financial protection for your loved ones in your absence. You can avail certain tax advantages too as per provisions stated in Income Tax Act, 1961. Disclaimer: The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read the sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajallianzlife.com) carefully before concluding a sale.
